paragraphs.body: To live in Louisiana means knowing The sultry sway of moss-draped oaks, The hum of cicadas as the sun dips low, And the sweet scent of gumbo that fills the air. And the sweet scent of gumbo that fills the air. To speak Creole means knowing The rhythm of a story told with heart, The blend of cultures that dance in the streets, And the joy of family gathered 'round the table. To live in Louisiana means to Embrace the swirl of festivals and song, To feel the pulse of jazz in your veins, And to savor life, one bite at a time. To speak to my brudda means to Have a great time with him Talk about everything get females with him My ‘home’ language is the language I speak with my friends, and cousins. Some of the things we say are “my lill yeah,” “boyy,” and “slut ahh boy.” We use the word “chill” when we feel somebody being annoying but I think everyone use chill, and we use “flonky” and like “wassam” yu can use “wassam” for like speaking to somebody or like Finna fight someone like “wassam” do some. For example, when i was at my game this dude almost hit me in my face i got mad and i was like “wassam” like do some, I was hot and i had to take a play of because I almost got kicked out of the game.

annotations.text: Talk about everything get females with him
annotations.comment: This feels a bit more authentic to me?

paragraphs.body: Brudda and Donkey are the languages I speak with my friends and family. It is a language of just what we created and what we thought was funny and we just started saying it and stuff for example we could be chillin or calling someone we be like, “wassup brudda.” Another example is like if one of my friends do something dumb, we will say to that person, “Donkey of the day.” We use different words, but some people cannot understand it.

annotations.text: and Donkey are the languages I speak with my friends and family.
annotations.comment: I'm interested in this language, too. But how is it related--for the purpose of an essay with one central focusing idea--to the "home" language above?

annotations.text: We use different words, but some people cannot understand
annotations.comment: Why is this language, this community, this closeness important to you?

paragraphs.body: One day i was out to get food and when I went I went to Canes on up behind my apartment building and when I went to order the cashier could not understand me and the words that I was using, and It kind of made me feel awkward because of his face expression when I'm talking he is so confuse about the situation. If i was in my hometown I would not have any problem with anyone struggling to understand what I am talking about. I was walking the street. I was with my Brudda. I seen this girl. I said to ha wassam luv. She said wassam back. Using my home language is another thing back home because everyone knows how everything is and the slang that we use across the whole state of Louisiana. Every sense I’ve moved here to Pittsburgh and meeting other people from other states and I’ve learned that other people have different languages and stuff, I have a roommate he is from Pittsburgh and they slang that he be using is crazy I always be like what does that mean and I be looking at him crazy because I never heard of what he is saying, and that’s the same for when I am around all of his friends and stuff they talk just like him. They use words like Nefs, Homiez, on Steve’s grave, 100it, words like this he say every single day and I also have another roommate that and his name is jaremiah and he is from Florida and he has a different kind of accent as well and we kinda act the same we are both from the south, but he still have a different slangs that he using such as, Jit, woah, when they say back they say bike I do not know why but he mostly uses jit. The word jit, you can use the word jit in so as many ways such as like the word we use wassam, like calling someone or like doing something with that person.

annotations.text: I was walking the street. I was with my Brudda. I seen this girl. I said to ha wassam luv. She said wassam back.
annotations.comment: I love this authenticity and voice, here.

paragraphs.body: When I came up here to Pittsburgh everything was different the streets the people you would see more people on the Side of the road walking and more homeless people than usual just everything around me is a big adjustment to school and how the teachers teach then how they teach back home but I am getting used to the adjustment and I’m starting figure the slang that people use down here.

annotations.text: When I came up here to Pittsburgh everything was different the streets the people you would see more people on the Side of the road walking and more homeless people than usual just everything around me is a big adjustment
annotations.comment: This is a great moment of friction. Let's talk!

paragraphs.body: Owen took me to his little brother high school football game, he plays for Oakland catholic and when I went as soon as I walked in the stadium I seen how everything so different from where I am from like how the coaches was calling plays and coaching on the sideline was very different from what my old high school coach’s did for me and my old teammates from back home. The coach’s had crazy slang and different accents to me I really couldn’t understand what they were saying, and this for me now at Pitt with my current coaches me getting to learn from my new coach’s and how they coach others and adapting to the new environment that I am in right now. For example, when I was back at home with my old high school team my old coach’s would scream at everything you do and I went to a private school so after everything we would pray and things like that, and that’s something for me as well that I’m getting used to as well when I was back home at my old school we had bible study everyday at 6:45 in the morning for 30 min and then go to practice I was literally in church everyday and that’s something I don’t have out here, that culture that had back home then now me being out here.

Even tho I am not down south in Louisiana and I do not have my home, and my home language, and all my friends and family with me, I am here now. I have been blessed by God to have the ability to adapt to being up north and discovering different people, and places and other things, and other slang that people use. Now that I am in college I will be meeting people all over the word and the language that they speak will be very interesting and I’m sure when they here my southern accent and the slang that I speak to them durning our conversation they will be amused and ready to here what I have to say when I am talking to them.

annotations.text: Even tho I am not down south in Louisiana and I do not have my home, and my home language, and all my friends and family with me, I am here now.
annotations.comment: This is also interesting. What has that change been like?
